name: OpenUSD Core Concepts
description: Fundamental concepts of Universal Scene Description including prims, properties, and stage hierarchy
key_facts: USD (Universal Scene Description) is an open-source framework for describing, composing, and rendering 3D scenes
A USD Stage is the top-level container that holds the composed scene graph from one or more layers
Prims (primitives) are the primary container objects in USD that form a hierarchical namespace
Properties on prims include attributes (typed data values) and relationships (connections to other prims)
USD files use .usda (ASCII text), .usdc (binary crate), and .usdz (zip archive) file formats
common_misconceptions: USD is just a 3D file format (it is a complete scene description framework with composition, schemas, and rendering capabilities)
USD can only represent static geometry (USD supports time-varying data through time-sampled attributes)

name: USD Paths and Namespaces
description: Understanding prim paths, property paths, and namespace hierarchy in USD
key_facts: Prim paths use forward-slash notation like /World/Characters/Hero to address prims in the hierarchy
Property paths append a dot-separated name to the prim path like /World/Characters/Hero.xformOp:translate
The pseudo-root prim at path / is the implicit root of every USD stage
Namespace operations like renaming or reparenting prims change their path and all descendant paths
Prim paths must be unique within a stage, enforcing a tree structure
common_misconceptions: USD paths work like file system paths in every way (USD paths have additional semantics like variant selections)
Renaming a prim only changes its name (it changes the path of all descendants and invalidates any relationships pointing to the old path)

name: USD Variant Sets
description: Using variant sets to represent multiple variations of a prim within a single asset
key_facts: Variant sets provide a way to package multiple variations (e.g., colors, LODs) of a prim in a single asset
A variant set contains named variants, and exactly one variant is selected at any time
Variant selections can be authored on any layer and participate in the composition arc ordering
Multiple variant sets can exist on a single prim for independent variation dimensions
Variant sets are commonly used for material assignments, geometric LODs, and regional variations
common_misconceptions: Variants duplicate all data for each variation (variants only override the properties that change between variations)
Variant selections are baked into the asset (selections can be overridden in stronger layers)

name: USD Composition Arcs
description: The six composition arc types and their strength ordering in the LIVRPS rule
key_facts: USD has six composition arcs ordered by strength: Local, Inherits, VariantSets, References, Payloads, Specializes (LIVRPS)
References compose one USD prim or layer into another, commonly used for asset assembly
Payloads are deferred references that can be unloaded to reduce memory usage for large scenes
Inherits provide class-based sharing where changes to the base class propagate to all inheriting prims
Specializes is the weakest arc, allowing a prim to provide fallback opinions that can be overridden
common_misconceptions: References and payloads are identical (payloads can be selectively loaded/unloaded, references are always loaded)
Composition order is arbitrary (the LIVRPS strength ordering is deterministic and critical for correct scene resolution)

name: USD Layer Stack and Opinions
description: How USD layers stack opinions and resolve values through the composition engine
key_facts: A USD layer is a file that contains authored opinions about prims and their properties
The layer stack determines opinion strength: sublayers listed earlier are stronger than later ones
The session layer is the strongest non-local layer, typically used for temporary edits that should not be saved
Value resolution traverses the composed layer stack and returns the strongest authored opinion
Sparse overrides allow layers to change only specific properties without reproducing the entire prim definition
common_misconceptions: Stronger layers delete weaker opinions (weaker opinions still exist and take effect if the stronger opinion is removed)
All layers must contain complete prim definitions (layers can contain sparse overrides affecting only specific properties)

name: USD Typed and API Schemas
description: Schema types in USD including typed schemas, API schemas, and custom schema creation
key_facts: Typed schemas (IsA schemas) define the type of a prim, such as UsdGeomMesh, UsdGeomXform, or UsdLuxDomeLight
API schemas add additional interfaces to prims without changing their type, such as UsdShadeMaterialBindingAPI
Single-apply API schemas can be applied once per prim; multi-apply API schemas can be applied multiple times with different instance names
Schema definitions are written in USD schema files and generate C++ and Python code via usdGenSchema
Fallback values in schemas provide default property values when no opinion is authored
common_misconceptions: A prim can have multiple typed schemas (each prim has exactly one type; additional behaviors come from API schemas)
Custom schemas require modifying the USD core (custom schemas are defined externally and registered as plugins)

name: Asset Structure and Data Modeling
description: Best practices for structuring USD assets including model hierarchy, kind, and purpose
key_facts: Model hierarchy uses Kind metadata to organize assets into assembly, group, component, and subcomponent levels
Components are the basic building blocks; assemblies compose components and groups into larger structures
Purpose metadata (default, render, guide, proxy) controls visibility for different workflows
Asset info metadata records authoring information like asset name, version, and identifier
Default prim designation specifies which prim should be used when the layer is referenced without a target path
common_misconceptions: Kind metadata affects rendering behavior (Kind is for organizational hierarchy; Purpose affects rendering visibility)
Every prim needs a Kind assignment (Kind is primarily used on model roots and key organizational prims)

name: USD Python API Development
description: Using the USD Python API for scene manipulation, asset processing, and pipeline tools
key_facts: Usd.Stage.Open() and Usd.Stage.CreateNew() are the primary entry points for working with USD stages in Python
UsdGeom module provides geometry-related schemas including Mesh, Xform, BasisCurves, and Camera
Sdf.Path class represents and manipulates USD prim and property paths
Stage traversal using Usd.PrimRange iterates over prims in the scene graph
Edit targets control which layer receives new opinions when modifying a composed stage
common_misconceptions: Changes to a composed stage are automatically saved (stages must be explicitly saved with stage.GetRootLayer().Save())
Python USD operations are slow for large scenes (many operations are implemented in C++ and called through Python bindings)

name: USD Pipeline Integration
description: Integrating USD into production pipelines for content creation, simulation, and rendering
key_facts: USD plugins extend functionality through file format plugins, schema plugins, and resolver plugins
Asset resolvers (ArResolver) customize how asset paths are resolved to actual file locations
USD Hydra is the rendering architecture that connects USD scene data to renderers like RenderMan, Arnold, and RTX
usdview is the reference USD viewer for inspecting and debugging USD scene composition
usdchecker validates USD files against schema compliance and best practices
common_misconceptions: USD requires a specific renderer (USD's Hydra architecture supports multiple renderers through render delegate plugins)
Pipeline integration requires rewriting existing tools (USD plugins and resolvers can bridge to existing asset management systems)

name: NVIDIA Omniverse Platform
description: NVIDIA Omniverse platform for building USD-based 3D workflows and collaborative applications
key_facts: NVIDIA Omniverse is a platform built on OpenUSD for building and operating industrial metaverse applications
Omniverse Nucleus provides a centralized server for real-time collaboration and live syncing of USD stages
Omniverse Kit is the SDK for building extensions and applications on the Omniverse platform
Omniverse Connectors bridge third-party DCC tools (Maya, Blender, 3ds Max) with Omniverse via USD
Omniverse RTX renderer provides real-time ray tracing and path tracing for USD scenes on NVIDIA GPUs
common_misconceptions: Omniverse replaces existing DCC tools (it connects and extends them through USD-based interoperability)
Omniverse requires cloud connectivity (it can run entirely on-premises with local Nucleus servers)
